elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper we establish some new results similar to Lagrange's four-square theorem.
elements.sent-2411-003: For example, we prove that any integer $n>1$ can be written $w(5w+1)/2+x(5x+1)/2+y(5y+1)/2+z(5z+1)/2$ with $w,x,y,z\in\mathbb Z$.
elements.sent-2411-004: Here we state two general results: (1) If $a$ and $b$ are odd integers with $a>0$, $b>-a$ and $\gcd(a,b)=1$, then all sufficient large integers can be written as \begin{equation}\frac{w(aw+b)}2+\frac{x(ax+b)}2+\frac{y(ay+b)}2+\frac{z(az+b)}2\end{equation} with $w,x,y,z$ nonnegative integers.
elements.sent-2411-005: For any integer $m\gs3$, the polygonal numbers of order $m$ (or $m$-gonal numbers) are those nonnegative integers \begin{equation}p_m(n)=(m-2)\bi n2+n=\f{n((m-2)n-(m-4))}2\ \ (n=0,1,2,\ldots).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-006: Those \begin{equation}\bar p_m(n)=p_m(-n)=\f{n((m-2)n+(m-4))}2\ \ (n=0,1,2,\ldots)\end{equation} are called second kind $m$-gonal numbers, and those $p_m(x)$ with $x\in\Z$ are called generalized $m$-gonal numbers.. Note that \begin{equation}p_3(x)=\f{x(x+1)}2,\ p_4(x)=x^2,\ p_5(x)=\f{x(3x-1)}2\ \t{and}\ p_8(x)=x(3x-2).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-007: Fermat claimed that each $n\in\N$ is a sum of $m$ $m$-gonal numbers; this was confirmed by Lagrange, Gauss and Cauchy for the cases $m=4$, $m=3$, and $m\gs5$, respectively (cf.. \cite{N87,N96 ).}
elements.sent-2411-008: In 2016 the author \cite{S16 proved that each $n\in\Z^+=\{1,2,3,\ldots\}$ can be written a sum of four generalized octagonal numbers one of which is odd.}
elements.sent-2411-009: This is quite similar to Lagrange's four-square theorem.
elements.sent-2411-010: In \cite{S18,S20 the author investigated those tuples $(a,b,c,d,e,f)\in\Z^6$ with $a-b,c-d,e-f$ all even such that each $n\in\N$ can be written as \begin{equation}\f{x(ax+b)}2+\f{y(cy+d)}2+\f{z(ez+f)}2\end{equation} with $x,y,z$ integers or nonnegative integers.}

elements.sent-2411-138: In 1638 Fermat claimed that each $n\in\N=\{0,1,2,\ldots\}$ is a sum of $m$ $m$-gonal numbers; this was confirmed by Lagrange, Gauss and Cauchy for the cases $m=4$, $m=3$, and $m\gs5$, respectively (cf.. \cite{N87,N96 ).}
elements.sent-2411-139: Lagrange's four-square theorem states that any $n\in\N$ can be written as $w^2+x^2+y^2+z^2$ with $w,x,y,z\in\N$.
elements.sent-2411-140: In 1830 Legendre refined Cauchy's polygonal number theorem by showing that for any integer $m\gs 5$ we can write each integer $N\gs 28(m-2)^3$ as \begin{equation}p_{m}(w)+p_{m}(x)+p_{m}(y)+p_{m}(z)+\da_m(N),\end{equation} where $w,x,y,z\in\N$, $\da_m(N)=0$ if $2\nmid m$, and $\da_m(N)\in\{0,1\}$ if $2\mid m$.

elements.sent-2411-144: In additive number theory, a subset $A$ of $\N$ is called a {\it base of order $n$} if \begin{equation}nA=\{a_1+\cdots+a_n:\ a_1,\ldots,a_n\in A\}\end{equation} coincides with $\N$.
elements.sent-2411-145: When $\N\sm nA$ is finite, we call $A$ an {\it asymptotic base of order $n$}.
